title: Fixed-Term Employees (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2002
chapters: SI 2002/2034 · Made 24 July 2002 · In Force 1 October 2002 · Implements Fixed-Term Work Directive 1999/70/EC
desc: The Fixed-Term Employees (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2002 protect fixed-term employees from less favourable treatment compared to comparable permanent employees, unless the treatment can be objectively justified. A fixed-term employee who has been continuously employed on successive fixed-term contracts for 4 or more years is deemed to be a permanent employee, unless the continued use of a fixed-term contract can be objectively justified. Fixed-term employees are entitled to a written statement of reasons for less favourable treatment on request. Dismissal by reason of exercising rights under the Regulations is automatically unfair. The Regulations implement the EU Fixed-Term Work Directive 1999/70/EC (Framework Agreement between ETUC, UNICE and CEEP).

parts.title: Less Favourable Treatment and Permanent Status

sections.title: Less favourable treatment of fixed-term employees
sections.summary: A fixed-term employee has the right not to be treated by the employer less favourably than the employer treats a comparable permanent employee in respect of the terms of their contract, or by being subjected to any other detriment, on the ground that the employee is a fixed-term employee, unless the treatment is objectively justified. Less favourable treatment may be justified on objective grounds if the terms of the fixed-term employee's contract, taken as a whole, are at least as favourable as those of the comparable permanent employee. The comparator must be employed by the same employer at the same establishment (or a different establishment if there is no comparator at the same establishment) doing the same or broadly similar work, with similar qualifications and skills.
sections.text:
(1) A fixed-term employee has the right not to be treated by his employer less favourably than the employer treats a comparable permanent employee—
(a) as regards the terms of his contract; or
(b) by being subjected to any other detriment by any act, or deliberate failure to act, of his employer.

(2) The right conferred by paragraph (1) applies only if—
(a) the treatment is on the ground that the employee is a fixed-term employee, and
(b) the treatment is not justified on objective grounds.

(3) The right conferred by paragraph (1) includes in particular the right of the fixed-term employee not to be treated less favourably than a comparable permanent employee in relation to—
(a) any period of service qualification relating to any particular condition of employment;
(b) the opportunity to receive training; or
(c) the opportunity to secure any permanent position in the establishment.

(5) In determining whether a fixed-term employee has been treated less favourably than a comparable permanent employee, the pro rata principle shall be applied unless it is inappropriate.

versions.description: Original provision implementing Framework Agreement clause 4 (non-discrimination). Unchanged since enactment.

leadingCases.name: Department for Work and Pensions v Webley [2005] ICR 577
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the non-renewal of a fixed-term contract is a dismissal for the purposes of the Regulations and ERA 1996, but the ground for the less favourable treatment is the expiry of the fixed term, not that the employee is a fixed-term employee. However, the treatment may still be challenged if the non-renewal is connected to the fixed-term status.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the relationship between non-renewal of fixed-term contracts and less favourable treatment under the Regulations

refs: reg.6 FTER 2002 (right to written statement of reasons for less favourable treatment)
ERA 1996 s.95(1)(b) (non-renewal of fixed-term contract as dismissal)

sections.title: Successive fixed-term contracts — the 4-year rule
sections.summary: Where an employee has been continuously employed on successive fixed-term contracts for a period of 4 years or more, the employee is deemed to be a permanent employee (employed under a contract of indefinite duration) from the date the 4-year period is completed, unless the employer can objectively justify the continued use of a fixed-term contract. A collective or workforce agreement may modify the 4-year period or substitute different conditions. The 4-year rule only counts service from 10 July 2002 (the date of the Directive's implementation deadline).
sections.text:
(1) This regulation applies where—
(a) an employee is employed under a contract purporting to be a fixed-term contract, and
(b) the contract mentioned in sub-paragraph (a) has previously been renewed, or the employee has previously been employed on a fixed-term contract before the start of the contract mentioned in sub-paragraph (a), and
(c) the employee has been continuously employed under the contract mentioned in sub-paragraph (a), or under that contract taken with a previous fixed-term contract, for a period of four years or more.

(2) Where this regulation applies, the provision of the contract mentioned in paragraph (1)(a) that restricts the duration of the contract shall be of no effect, and the employee shall be a permanent employee, if the employment of the employee under a fixed-term contract was not justified on objective grounds—
(a) where the contract mentioned in paragraph (1)(a) has been renewed, at the time when it was last renewed;
(b) where that contract has not been renewed, at the time when it was entered into.

versions.description: Original provision implementing Framework Agreement clause 5 (prevention of abuse). The 4-year threshold and objective justification defence have not been amended since enactment.

leadingCases.name: Duncombe v Secretary of State for Children, Schools and Families [2011] UKSC 36
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court considered the application of the 4-year rule and held that the use of fixed-term contracts must be objectively justified. The fact that the employer had a genuine reason for using a fixed-term contract at each renewal does not necessarily mean that the continued use after 4 years is justified. The tribunal must assess the justification at the point when the 4-year threshold is reached.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority on objective justification for continued use of fixed-term contracts beyond 4 years

refs: Fixed-Term Work Directive 1999/70/EC (Framework Agreement clause 5 — measures to prevent abuse)
ERA 1996 s.95(1)(b) (expiry of fixed term as dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.98(1)(b) (some other substantial reason — may justify non-renewal)

pendingAmendments: Post-Brexit — FTER 2002 now assimilated law under REUL Act 2023
No ERA 2025 amendments to FTER directly, but removal of unfair dismissal qualifying period (s.1) will affect fixed-term employee dismissal claims

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: European Communities Act 1972 s.2(2) and Employment Act 2002
parliamentaryOrigin.euDirective: Fixed-Term Work Directive 1999/70/EC (Framework Agreement between ETUC, UNICE and CEEP)
parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The FTER 2002 implemented the Fixed-Term Work Directive 1999/70/EC. The Directive's Framework Agreement (negotiated between EU-level social partners ETUC, UNICE, and CEEP) established two core principles: (1) non-discrimination against fixed-term workers (clause 4), and (2) prevention of abuse through successive fixed-term contracts (clause 5). The UK implemented clause 5 through the 4-year rule in reg.8.

precedingDocuments.title: Fixed-Term Work Directive 1999/70/EC
precedingDocuments.significance: The governing EU Directive incorporating the Framework Agreement on fixed-term work. Required non-discrimination (clause 4) and abuse prevention through successive contracts (clause 5).

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Act 2002
precedingDocuments.significance: Provided additional enabling powers for the FTER 2002 alongside the ECA 1972.

leadingCases.name: Duncombe v Secretary of State for Children, Schools and Families [2011] UKSC 36
leadingCases.heldText: Objective justification for continued use of fixed-term contracts beyond 4 years must be assessed at the point the threshold is reached.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority on the 4-year rule and objective justification.

leadingCases.name: DWP v Webley [2005] ICR 577
leadingCases.heldText: Non-renewal of a fixed-term contract is a dismissal but the ground is expiry of the term, not the fixed-term status itself.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified relationship between non-renewal and less favourable treatment.
